claimed_contribution: The paper shows that the usual similarity advantage of copycats can reverse: under comparative evaluation, moderate-similarity copycats can be evaluated more positively than high-similarity copycats.

It identifies consumers' evaluation mode (comparative vs. noncomparative) as a key moderator of copycat effectiveness, and shows this mode can be shifted by leader-brand presence at evaluation or by direct instructions.
It argues that subtle copycats can free ride on leader-brand equity without causing confusion and therefore may be both practically and legally more problematic than blatant copycats.
context: The paper studies copycat branding in grocery shopping settings where follower brands imitate leader brands through brand names or package design. Empirical contexts include olive oil, spreadable butter with olive oil, chocolate spread, and French cream cheese, with leader brands such as Bertolli, Nutella, and Paturain.
design_and_data: The authors run three controlled experiments that manipulate copycat-leader similarity and evaluation mode (or leader-brand presence as a situational trigger of mode). Study 1a (N = 50 students) and Study 1b (N = 44 students after exclusions) use olive-oil brand names similar to Bertolli; Study 1b directly induces comparative vs. noncomparative evaluation instructions. Study 2 (N = 65 students) uses professionally designed butter package stimuli at low, moderate, and high similarity to Bertolli. Study 3 uses a nationally representative household internet panel (N = 542) with brand-name stimuli in two categories (chocolate spread and French cream cheese) and measures both evaluation and willingness to buy.
main_findings: When evaluation is noncomparative or the leader brand is absent, higher similarity tends to improve copycat evaluations, with high-similarity copycats performing best.

When evaluation is comparative or the leader brand is present, high-similarity copycats lose their advantage and are evaluated less favorably, while moderate-similarity copycats are evaluated more positively than both high- and low-similarity copycats in Studies 2 and 3.

Across brand-name and package-design manipulations, student and nonstudent samples, and in Study 3 also willingness-to-buy outcomes, high-similarity copycats are more negatively affected by comparative evaluation than moderate-similarity copycats.
one_line_summary: Across three experiments, the paper shows that high-similarity copycats benefit in noncomparative evaluation but lose to moderate-similarity copycats when consumers compare them with the leader brand.
relation_to_prior_work: challenges the prevailing copycat and trademark view that greater similarity is uniformly more dangerous by showing that this effect is contingent on evaluation mode. It demonstrates that comparative evaluation can produce contrast against high-similarity copycats while leaving moderate-similarity copycats relatively advantaged.
research_gap: Prior work and legal focus largely emphasized blatant, high-similarity copycats and brand confusion, with less attention to subtle, moderate-similarity imitation. The paper addresses the unresolved question of when similarity helps versus hurts copycats by incorporating consumers' comparative versus noncomparative evaluation mode.
research_question: How does the degree of similarity between a copycat and a leader brand affect copycat evaluation, and does this depend on whether consumers evaluate comparatively or noncomparatively? Specifically, can moderate-similarity copycats outperform high-similarity copycats when consumers directly compare copycats with the leader brand?
